title: California Driver License – Generation 3 REAL ID
subtitle: Issued from approximately 2018–present; bears federal REAL ID star mark
sources: REAL ID Act of 2005, 49 U.S.C. § 30301 note
6 CFR Part 37 – Minimum Standards for Driver's Licenses and Identification Cards Acceptable by Federal Agencies for Official Purposes
California DMV – California DL/ID Card Design Specification (Gen 3, 2018)
ISO/IEC 7816-1:2011 – Identification cards – Integrated circuit cards

sections.title: Generation & Variant Identification
hints: Does the card display a gold five-pointed star in the upper-right corner of the front face?
The REAL ID star is mandatory for REAL ID-compliant cards; its absence indicates either a non-compliant card or a Gen 1/2 issue.

hints.source: 6 CFR Part 37 – Minimum Standards for Driver's Licenses and Identification Cards
hints.quote: A REAL ID compliant driver's license or identification card must display a star in the upper portion of the card.

hints: Is the star gold (not silver, white, or black) and printed at full opacity without visible banding?
California uses a gold star; color-shifted or translucent stars may indicate a counterfeit.

hints.source: California DMV – California DL/ID Card Design Specification (Gen 3, 2018)
hints.quote: The REAL ID star shall be printed in gold ink at the upper-right corner of the card face.

hints: Does the card header read 'CALIFORNIA' above 'DRIVER LICENSE' (or 'IDENTIFICATION CARD') in the correct state-mandated font?
Gen 3 California DLs use a specific font and uppercase styling; mixed case or alternate wording is a red flag.

hints.quote: The card header shall display the full state name and document type in the format specified by the issuing state's design specification.

hints: Is the card layout consistent with the Gen 3 portrait orientation (photo on the left, data fields on the right)?
California Gen 3 retains a portrait-style layout; Gen 1/2 used a different photo placement.

hints.source: California DMV – California DL/ID Card Design Specification (Gen 3, 2018)
hints.quote: The Gen 3 California DL/ID card places the primary portrait photograph at the left and data fields at the right of the front face.

sections.title: Physical Card Dimensions & Substrate
hints: Are the card dimensions 85.6 mm × 53.98 mm (±0.12 mm tolerance) conforming to ISO/IEC 7810 ID-1 format?
Non-standard dimensions immediately suggest a counterfeit or altered document.

hints.quote: ID-1 cards shall have nominal dimensions of 85.60 mm × 53.98 mm with a thickness of 0.76 mm ±0.08 mm.

hints: Cards that are noticeably thicker or thinner than 0.76 mm may be counterfeit.

hints.quote: Nominal thickness of an ID-1 card shall be 0.76 mm with a permissible deviation of ±0.08 mm.

hints: Does the card flex without cracking under moderate pressure and return to flat (polycarbonate construction)?
California Gen 3 cards are polycarbonate; paper-laminate counterfeits will crack at lower flex angles.

hints.quote: Polycarbonate cards shall resist cracking and delamination under the flexing test specified in ISO/IEC 7810.

hints: Are all card corners uniformly rounded to a radius of 3.18 mm per ISO/IEC 7810?
Sharp or irregular corners suggest non-compliant substrate.

hints.quote: The corners of ID-1 cards shall be rounded with a radius of 3.18 mm.

sections.title: Document Number & Issuer Identification
hints: Does the document number begin with a single uppercase alpha character followed by 7 digits (e.g., A1234567)?
California DL numbers follow an alpha-numeric format: 1 letter + 7 digits. Non-conforming formats are invalid.

hints.source: California DMV – California DL/ID Card Design Specification (Gen 3, 2018)
hints.quote: California driver license numbers consist of one alpha character followed by seven numeric digits.

hints: Is 'CA' printed as the issuing jurisdiction abbreviation visible on the card face?
California-issued cards must display the 2-letter ANSI state abbreviation.

hints.quote: The issuing jurisdiction's two-letter ANSI code shall be displayed on the card face.

hints: Is the document number laser-engraved into the card body (visible as a tactile indentation rather than surface ink)?
California Gen 3 laser-engraves the DL number; surface-printed numbers indicate counterfeit.

hints.source: California DMV – California DL/ID Card Design Specification (Gen 3, 2018)
hints.quote: The document number shall be laser-engraved into the polycarbonate card body for tamper resistance.

sections.title: Dates (Issue, Expiration, Date of Birth)
hints: Are dates displayed in MM/DD/YYYY format on the card face?
California DL dates are displayed in MM/DD/YYYY; other formats (DD/MM/YYYY or YYYY-MM-DD) indicate a fraudulent document.

hints.quote: Dates on the card face shall be displayed in MM/DD/YYYY format for U.S.-issued cards.

hints: Is the expiration date consistent with California's standard 5-year or 8-year renewal cycle depending on age at issuance?
California issues 5-year licenses to drivers under 70 and 2-year licenses to drivers 70+. Expiration intervals outside this range are suspicious.

hints.quote: A driver's license shall expire on the fifth birthday of the licensee following the date of the application, except for applicants 70 or older who receive a 2-year term.

hints: Is the date of birth printed in the same MM/DD/YYYY format and visually consistent (same font, weight, size) as other date fields?
Inconsistent font metrics on DOB relative to other dates may indicate field alteration.

hints.quote: All dates on the card face shall use a uniform typeface and size as specified by the issuing authority's design specification.

hints: Does the issue date (ISS) pre-date the expiration date (EXP) by exactly the term prescribed by state law?
A mismatch in the issue-to-expiration interval is a strong fraud indicator.

hints.source: 6 CFR Part 37 – Minimum Standards for Driver's Licenses
hints.quote: Each REAL ID driver's license must include an expiration date and an issue date on the card face.

sections.title: Physical Descriptors
hints: Is the holder's height displayed in feet-and-inches format (e.g., '5-09') on the card face?
California encodes height as FT-IN; metric-only display suggests a non-genuine document.

hints.quote: Height shall be displayed in feet and inches (FT-IN) format for U.S.-issued driver's licenses.

hints: Is the eye color abbreviation one of the AAMVA-defined codes (BLK, BLU, BRN, GRY, GRN, HAZ, MAR, PNK, DIC)?
Non-standard eye color codes not in the AAMVA lookup table indicate tampering.

hints.quote: Eye color shall be coded using the two- or three-letter abbreviations defined in AAMVA Table D4.

hints: Is the sex field coded as 'M', 'F', or 'X' (California added nonbinary 'X' option in 2019)?
Post-2019 California cards may show 'X' for nonbinary. Codes outside M/F/X are invalid.

hints.quote: Commencing January 1, 2019, the department shall offer applicants the option to select 'male,' 'female,' or 'nonbinary' as their gender on their driver's license.

hints: Is the hair color abbreviation one of the AAMVA-defined codes (BAL, BLK, BLN, BRO, GRY, RED, SDY, WHI, UNK)?
Non-standard hair color codes not in AAMVA Table D3 indicate tampering or fraud.

hints.quote: Hair color shall be coded using the three-letter abbreviations defined in AAMVA Table D3.

sections.title: Portrait & Ghost Image
hints: Is the primary portrait laser-engraved into the card body (not a surface-adhered photograph)?
Polycarbonate Gen 3 cards have laser-engraved images; peeling, bubbling, or visible photo edges indicate substitution.

hints.quote: The primary portrait shall be personalized directly into the card substrate using laser engraving or equivalent tamper-evident technology.

hints: Is a smaller secondary 'ghost' portrait present on the card face?
California Gen 3 includes a ghost (repeat) image as a security feature; its absence is anomalous.

hints.source: California DMV – California DL/ID Card Design Specification (Gen 3, 2018)
hints.quote: A secondary ghost portrait at reduced scale shall appear on the card face as an additional anti-counterfeiting measure.

hints: Does the ghost image match the primary portrait (same person, same pose)?
Mismatched ghost and primary images indicate photo substitution fraud.

hints.source: California DMV – California DL/ID Card Design Specification (Gen 3, 2018)
hints.quote: The ghost portrait shall be derived from the same digital image source as the primary portrait to ensure matching.

hints: Is the portrait background a California state-themed pattern (not solid white or generic gradient)?
California Gen 3 uses a state-specific background pattern behind the portrait; solid or generic backgrounds suggest counterfeit.

hints.source: California DMV – California DL/ID Card Design Specification (Gen 3, 2018)
hints.quote: The portrait area background shall incorporate California-specific design elements as specified in the Gen 3 card design.

sections.title: Visual Security Features
hints: Is there a optically variable device (OVD) or holographic laminate visible on the card surface?
California Gen 3 incorporates an OVD/holographic layer; absence indicates a flat-printed counterfeit.

hints.quote: Cards should incorporate an OVD or holographic element as a Level 1 overt security feature.

hints: Does the OVD/hologram shift color and/or pattern when tilted at different angles under a single light source?
Static or single-color holograms are inconsistent with genuine OVD technology.

hints.quote: The OVD shall exhibit color-shift or image-switch effects when the viewing angle is changed.

hints: Is a fine-line guilloche background pattern visible across the data zone of the card face?
California Gen 3 uses a fine-line guilloche as a printing security feature; absence or crude substitution indicates counterfeit.

hints.source: California DMV – California DL/ID Card Design Specification (Gen 3, 2018)
hints.quote: A guilloché pattern shall be printed in the background of the data zone to deter photocopying and scanning.

hints: Is microprinting present and legible under 10× magnification in designated security areas (e.g., along the portrait border or state name)?
Microprint that is blurred or absent at 10× magnification is a key counterfeit indicator.

hints.quote: Microprinting shall be incorporated in security-critical areas and must be legible under magnification as a Level 2 covert security feature.

hints: Is there a laser-perforated state outline or design element visible when the card is held to light?
California Gen 3 uses laser perforation; absence suggests an older generation or counterfeit.

hints.source: California DMV – California DL/ID Card Design Specification (Gen 3, 2018)
hints.quote: A laser-perforated California state outline shall be incorporated into the card body as a Level 2 security feature.

hints: Is a color-shifting ink (CSI) or color-shifting security element present on the card?
CSI elements visible when tilting the card are security features; flat, non-shifting color indicates absence of this feature.

hints.quote: Color-shifting ink or similar optically variable security elements should be incorporated as an overt security feature.

hints: Does the card bear an 'UNDER 21 UNTIL MM/DD/YYYY' notice if the holder is currently under 21?
California law requires age notation for under-21 holders; absence on a card for a minor is anomalous.

hints.quote: The department shall issue a distinctive driver's license to persons under 21 years of age that contains the notation 'UNDER 21 UNTIL' followed by the date the person will turn 21.

sections.title: UV / Fluorescent Features
hints: Under 365 nm UV illumination, does the card background reveal UV-reactive security printing not visible in white light?
Genuine California Gen 3 cards have UV-fluorescent inks in the background; absence indicates counterfeit.

hints.quote: Cards should incorporate UV-fluorescent elements visible under 365 nm ultraviolet light as a Level 2 security feature.

hints: Under UV, does the portrait area fluoresce distinctly from the surrounding card body?
Uniform UV fluorescence across portrait and background may indicate an overlay substitution.

hints.source: California DMV – California DL/ID Card Design Specification (Gen 3, 2018)
hints.quote: The portrait zone shall exhibit a distinct UV fluorescence pattern that differs from the card background under 365 nm UV illumination.

hints: Does the UV pattern reveal the California state seal or state-specific artwork?
State-specific UV artwork is a high-fidelity security feature difficult to counterfeit.

hints.source: California DMV – California DL/ID Card Design Specification (Gen 3, 2018)
hints.quote: California-specific UV artwork including state seal elements shall be incorporated into the UV security layer.

hints: Under UV, is there an absence of fluorescence from the document number and biographical text (laser-engraved elements do not fluoresce)?
Fluorescent ink on laser-engraved data fields indicates that data was surface-printed post-issuance.

hints.quote: Laser-engraved personalization shall not fluoresce under UV illumination, distinguishing it from surface-printed additions.

sections.title: Card Back & 2D Barcode
hints: Is a PDF417 2D barcode present on the card back?
All AAMVA-compliant U.S. DL/IDs must carry a PDF417 barcode on the back.

hints.quote: All jurisdictions issuing driver licenses and identification cards must incorporate a PDF417 barcode on the back of the card.

hints: Does the PDF417 barcode decode without errors using a standard barcode reader?
A barcode that fails to decode entirely may indicate a non-AAMVA-compliant or counterfeit card.

hints.quote: The PDF417 barcode shall be readable by commercially available 2D barcode readers without the need for proprietary software.

hints: Does the barcode file header begin with the AAMVA-compliant subfile designator '@\n\x1e\rANSI ' or '@\n\x1e\rAAMVA'?
Non-AAMVA header strings indicate a spoofed or non-standard barcode payload.

hints.quote: The data content of the PDF417 barcode shall begin with the compliance indicator '@', followed by the data element separator, record separator, carriage return, and the issuer identification number header.

hints: Does the barcode encode 'CA' as the issuing jurisdiction code in the IIN/AAMVAIssuerIdentificationNumber field?
A mismatched issuer code in the barcode versus the printed card face is a strong fraud indicator.

hints.quote: The IIN shall identify the issuing jurisdiction; California is assigned code 636014.

hints: Is a 1D Code 39 or Code 128 magnetic stripe or linear barcode also present on the card back?
California Gen 3 does not use a magnetic stripe; presence of one is anomalous for this generation.

hints.source: California DMV – California DL/ID Card Design Specification (Gen 3, 2018)
hints.quote: The Gen 3 California DL/ID card does not incorporate a magnetic stripe; only a PDF417 barcode is used for machine-readable data.

hints: Is the PDF417 barcode orientation horizontal (landscape) and placed in the lower half of the card back?
AAMVA specifies placement requirements for the PDF417 barcode.

hints.quote: The PDF417 barcode shall be placed in a horizontal orientation in the lower portion of the card back.

sections.title: Cross-Field Consistency (Front vs. Barcode)
hints: Does the document number printed on the front (DAQ field) match the DAQ field decoded from the PDF417 barcode exactly?
DAQ is the AAMVA field code for Customer ID Number (the DL number). A mismatch is definitive fraud evidence.

hints.quote: DAQ – Customer ID Number: The number assigned or calculated by the issuing authority. Must match the document number printed on the card face.

hints: Does the last name printed on the front (DCS field) match the DCS field in the barcode?
DCS is the AAMVA field code for Customer Family Name. Any discrepancy between printed and barcode surname indicates alteration.

hints.quote: DCS – Customer Family Name: Family name of the cardholder. Shall match the surname as printed on the card face.

hints: Does the date of birth printed on the front (MM/DD/YYYY) match the DBB barcode field when converted from MMDDCCYY format?
DBB is the AAMVA field code for Date of Birth, encoded MMDDCCYY in the barcode. A discrepancy indicates date-of-birth fraud.

hints.quote: DBB – Date of Birth: The date of birth of the cardholder, encoded as MMDDCCYY. Must correspond to the DOB printed on the card face.

hints: Does the expiration date printed on the front match the DBA barcode field (encoded as MMDDCCYY)?
DBA is the AAMVA field code for Document Expiration Date. Discrepancy indicates document alteration.

hints.quote: DBA – Document Expiration Date: Date on which the driving and identification privileges granted by the document are no longer valid, encoded as MMDDCCYY.

hints: Does the street address printed on the front (DAG field) match the DAG barcode field?
DAG is the AAMVA field code for Street Address 1. Mismatches between printed and barcode address indicate alteration.

hints.quote: DAG – Address – Street 1: Street portion of the cardholder's address. Shall match the address printed on the card face.

hints: Does the city printed on the front (DAI field) match the DAI barcode field?
DAI is the AAMVA field code for Address City. A city name discrepancy is a fraud indicator.

hints.quote: DAI – Address – City: City portion of the cardholder's address. Must match the city as printed on the card face.

hints: Does the sex code printed on the front match the DBC barcode field (1=Male, 2=Female, 9=Not Specified)?
DBC is the AAMVA field code for Sex. California Gen 3 encodes 'X'/nonbinary as 9. Mismatch indicates alteration.

hints.quote: DBC – Sex: 1 = male; 2 = female; 9 = not specified. Shall correspond to the sex as printed on the card face.

hints: Does the eye color printed on the front match the DAY barcode field?
DAY is the AAMVA field code for Eye Color. Any discrepancy indicates data alteration.

hints.quote: DAY – Eye Color: Codes for the primary color of the cardholder's eye(s). Must match the eye color abbreviation on the card face.

hints: Does the ZIP code printed on the front (DAK field) match the DAK barcode field?
DAK is the AAMVA field code for ZIP Code. Mismatches indicate address field alteration.

hints.quote: DAK – Address – Postal Code: Postal code portion of the cardholder's address, left-justified. Must match the ZIP code on the card face.

hints: Does the state abbreviation in the address on the front (DAJ field) match the DAJ barcode field and equal 'CA'?
DAJ is the AAMVA field code for Address State. If the barcode encodes a state other than CA, the card is fraudulent.

hints.quote: DAJ – Address – State: State portion of the cardholder's address. For California-issued cards this value shall be 'CA'.

sections.title: REAL ID Compliance Checks
hints: Does the card bear a machine-readable zone or compliance marker indicating federal acceptance (REAL ID star)?
Federal REAL ID compliance requires the star marker; TSA-compliant documents must display it.

hints.source: 6 CFR Part 37 – Minimum Standards for Driver's Licenses
hints.quote: Each REAL ID compliant driver's license or identification card must display a star marking to indicate compliance.

hints: Does the card NOT display 'NOT FOR FEDERAL IDENTIFICATION' or 'FEDERAL LIMITS APPLY' (which would indicate non-REAL ID compliance)?
Non-compliant California cards display a federal limits notice. A genuine REAL ID card omits this notice.

hints.source: 6 CFR Part 37 – Minimum Standards for Driver's Licenses
hints.quote: Cards not meeting REAL ID requirements must be marked to indicate they are not acceptable for federal identification purposes.

hints: Is the full legal name of the holder displayed (not a truncated or nickname form)?
REAL ID regulations require the full legal name as it appears on the source document used for identity verification.

hints.source: 6 CFR Part 37 – Minimum Standards for Driver's Licenses
hints.quote: The driver's license or identification card must contain the cardholder's full legal name.

hints: Is the principal address displayed (P.O. Box addresses alone are not acceptable for REAL ID)?
REAL ID requires a residential address; P.O. Box-only addresses are not permitted.

hints.source: 6 CFR Part 37 – Minimum Standards for Driver's Licenses
hints.quote: The driver's license or identification card must display the cardholder's principal residence address, not a post office box.

sections.title: Signature
hints: Is a digitized signature of the holder present on the card face?
California REAL ID cards include the holder's digitized signature as a mandatory element.

hints.source: 6 CFR Part 37 – Minimum Standards for Driver's Licenses
hints.quote: The REAL ID compliant card must display the cardholder's signature.

hints: Is the signature laser-engraved or otherwise personalized into the card body (not a wet-ink or sticker signature)?
Polycarbonate California Gen 3 cards personalize the signature digitally; surface-applied signatures indicate fraud.

hints.source: California DMV – California DL/ID Card Design Specification (Gen 3, 2018)
hints.quote: The digitized cardholder signature shall be laser-engraved into the card body.

hints: Is the signature field blank only for holders who are unable to sign (e.g., minors or persons with disabilities), with appropriate notation?
A blank signature without notation on a standard adult DL is suspicious.

hints.source: California DMV – California DL/ID Card Design Specification (Gen 3, 2018)
hints.quote: If a cardholder is unable to provide a signature, the signature field may be annotated with an appropriate exception code.

sections.title: Overall Integrity & Tamper Evidence
hints: Are all printed data fields free of visible alterations, scraping, chemical bleaching, or reapplied ink?
Any visible alteration to personalized data fields is a definitive fraud indicator.

hints.quote: Genuine cards shall show no evidence of physical or chemical alteration to any personalized data element.

hints: Is the card free of delamination, bubbling, or separation of the laminate layers?
Delamination is a primary indicator of photo substitution or data field tampering on polycarbonate cards.

hints.quote: The card laminate shall show no signs of delamination, bubbling, or edge separation under normal handling.

hints: Are the edges of the card clean and uniform without signs of cutting, re-lamination, or insertion of additional layers?
Re-laminated or composite counterfeit cards often exhibit edge irregularities.

hints.quote: Card edges shall be smooth and uniform; any roughness, layering, or irregularity may indicate a counterfeit or altered document.

hints: Does the card feel uniform and rigid when gently flexed, without soft spots or internal separations?
Soft spots or internal separations suggest delamination or insertion of foreign materials.

hints.quote: Cards shall withstand the specified flexing test without physical separation of layers or permanent deformation.

hints: Are all security features (hologram, microprint, UV elements, laser engraving) mutually consistent and showing no signs of misalignment?
Counterfeit cards frequently exhibit misalignment between security layers applied separately.

hints.quote: All security elements shall be precisely registered to the card design; misalignment between any features is a counterfeit indicator.

hints: Does the card pass a 'feel test' — is the surface texture and tactile quality consistent with a genuine polycarbonate card (not glossy paper or PVC sheet)?
Genuine polycarbonate cards have a distinctive tactile quality; paper or thin PVC counterfeits feel noticeably different.

hints.quote: Cards constructed of polycarbonate shall have surface characteristics consistent with the material specification, distinguishable from paper or PVC substrates.
